item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Random SCP" brought me here, so I gave it a bit of a quick read.

+ some general stuff
- hide

Unfortunately, I'm not too sure if it managed to maintain my interest consistently throughout the article. My inner sentiments mostly echo what not_a_seagull had said; the long blocks of text of describing the anomaly and the messy order in which the explanation of what each SCP designation is arranged in pretty much threw me off, I think. There are one or two spelling mistakes, and quite a bit of less clinical bits that I think could be cleared up with some proofreading.

The interview didn't add a lot of narrative value aside from them being tied to Norse mythology. Like, the runes on the tree and on the ravens, for some reason, kinda hints at something mythological here, and especially Norse? So it didn't really come across as a huge surprise during the interview when the ravens told the researcher their job is to collect secrets for Odin. There wasn't a big build-up to a climax here, which I think is also one of the primary concerns. Or, I think it was building up to something during the interview, but then it… ends? With no climax or something to make us go "oh shit". It's not bad that sometimes stuff can be a bit lengthy, but in this case, the less clinical bits plus the rather confusing order in which SCP designation is arranged in kinda made me lose interest quite a bit, and the ending didn't feel… like it had a payoff. -1.

+ some very nitpicky stuff
- hide

MTF Theta-8 are to be relocated

I usually consider an MTF unit as a collective term, so I usually say "is" instead of "are" unless you're saying MTF operatives?

Any Foundation personnel 03 or below must receive permission

I think I get what you're saying here for the clearance level, but the way this was structured was… a little weird?

D-Class personnel are to under no circumstance be allowed to communicate with SCP-4580.

Under no circumstance are personnel to attack SCP-4580.

Hm. I'm not sure if these two should be on their own, cuz it kinda gives a bit of a misconception that they're really important bits to the climax, which in this case, there isn't really one?

immediatly

"immediately"

If questioned, personnel are to state one of the following:

This bit felt… a little extraneous? It could be summed up to basically telling the public that something's wrong with the tree or it's designated as a wildlife conservation effort in a single sentence instead of giving overly-detailed stuff like this?

Any further excuses

"excuses" feels a bit of a weird word choice in terms of clinical tone?

at least externally

The sentence in which this bit is in felt… a little too informal?

generally leave SCP-4580-4 at some point during the day, generally returning five to ten hours afterword

The double "generally" feels a little iffy here, imo.

It is believed that they are listening in on people's conversations and collecting secrets during this time.

Narrative-wise, it feels weird for the Foundation to directly state this as if they knew all about what the ravens did when realistically speaking, I'm… not sure how they managed to know that they eavesdrop on people's conversations for secrets?

(the tree that SCP-4580 are currently located on)

I think this could be stated after the very first mention of SCP-4580-4?

They will then transform into humanoid figures.

I feel that this bit could be moved to after you say that there's a big cloud of feathers to get that tension up better?

SCP-4580-3 will be the opposite.

This felt a little weird in terms of clinical tone as well? "Inverted", maybe?

There wasn't much of what the -2 and -3 were doing after they cawed in the event the subject's aggressive to them?

will manifest carved

Hm, it feels weird for it to be together like this. Maybe a comma in between?

When translated, these runes say, "██████ ███████ ██████ ███████, ████ ██████████ ██ ██."

Why is this redacted?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
not_a_seagull,

Thank you for your comment! I just want to adress a few things:

1. Their ravens, not swans.
2. I will try to trim this as much as I can, maybe add a test log.
3. The main reason I added a lot of what I did was to I guess confirm the ties to Norse mythology.

Anyway, I will try to make all suggested changes, however, I am working on other things at the same time, so these changes probably won't be instant. Goodbye!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your description, in my opinion, is way too long. This is a problem for a lot of first skips; it feels necessary to elucidate the behavior as thoroughly as possible. However, the end result is a very long block of text that I found myself skimming over to get the actual relevant information. I downvoted primarily for this.

If you would like to fix this, I would recommend identifying your core concept, which in this instance seems to be "swans that find and sell secrets." Then, go over your Description, identify information that is not immediately crucial for this concept and cut it. If you feel wishy-washy about anything, you could use a test log.

This segues into my final problem with this skip: it feels like nothing happens. You describe the birds, then you describe where they came from in an interview, and nothing else really happens. It almost feels like I'm reading a textbook about the SCP, rather than a dynamic story about an SCP. Again, test logs, incident logs, or even a more active interview log would resolve this issue.

However, this definitely needs more work. -1

item.title: Image Credit
item.content:encoded: Image credit now goes to Laneous. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That link shows that you need to buy the image, and it has "all rights reserved" at the bottom, which makes it non-compliant. This image is under Wikipedia Commons, if you wish you can use it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New image source: https://videohive.net/item/ravens-in-tree-7/3400253

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
While I thank you for sourcing the photo, the source in question is still non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0) because shutterstock's license allows for the use of their photos in non-commercial works only, AKA "NC", which violates the SCP wiki's license. Thus, the image has been taken down from the article.

EDIT: A new cc-compliant image has been put up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Image Source: https://www.shutterstock.com/video/search/odins-ravens

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quasibenevolence,

Thank you for your input. I'm sorry I didn't get to you sooner, I have been very busy. Anyway, for one, I will try to clarify that SCP-4580-4 is a tree earlier in the article. Secondly, I have had several requestes to redo the interview log, so that will be coming soon. Thank you again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for catching that, the change has been made!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't describe an object as a subset of the scp designation (SCP-Z-X, where X is the subset) paragraphs after introducing the object. 4580-4 is a tree. Tell me it's a tree when you introduce 4580-4, not ages after.

Straight-up: the Foundation wouldn't exchange Foundation secrets for secrets. If there is ever a breach, the Foundation would have just handed whoever reached this object the secrets of the Foundation. The Foundation would either dictate terms or, if possible, negotiate for some precious commodity. This isn't an appeal to canon, just good sense. If I have a ton of cash and a lot of dangerous secrets, I'll trade in cash, not secrets. If I'm dirt poor, then and only then do you find out what happened at Band Camp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is an SCP-XXXX in the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
MathBrush,

Thank you for your critisism! Anyway, I'm sorry I didn't respond until now, as I was busy with school. I will try to implament all the requested changes as I feel necassary. Thank you again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to no-vote this one. The concept is interesting, but the execution feels 'off' to me.

Specifically, I feel like the Foundation isn't responding realistically here. They go into great detail about the appearance and behavior of the crows, and just throw in 'and then transforms into a human' as if transforming is a simple thing requiring no explanation.

Similarly, in the conversation, immediately agreeing to the Raven's terms, referring to them by name and offering them secrets without any thought. I feel like, if the Foundation respected them, they would not refer to them by SCP-4580 to begin with, and if they didn't respect them, they would keep using SCP-4580. And I feel like they would need to consult with their superiors.

I feel like the boring is dragged out and the awesome is rushed over, so many fine-tuning that would be good, and could help with that 'hook' Researcher WTD mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So after initially just kind of looking at this long wall of text I skipped over it and went on to different ones but today i finally got to reading and it honestly is very interesting.

I think the biggest weakness of your article is that it needs a stronger hook to keep the viewer reading up to the more interesting parts. Or you could try to split up the giant description block a bit so it's less intimidating.

Overall a very enjoyable read though after getting to the good parts so +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Generic_Viking,

I will be changing the class from safe to euclid. Thank you for your input!

item.title: Re: Notes on 4580
item.content:encoded:
CryonicAutum,

First of all, thank you for changing the "SCP-XXXX"s that I didn't catch. I appreciate that. Second of all, I will try to improve the containment proceedures. Thank you very much for your feedback!

item.title: Notes on 4580
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept of Odin's Ravens in-universe with the exchange of secrets coming into play and think that this has quite a bit of potential. I do think your clinical tone needs a bit more work, but the concept is done well enough that it stands as its own unique thing. I would recommend feeding the text into a text-to-speech program to refine it (I find that hearing your own writing can be helpful). Currently, I think that the Special Containment Procedures are the weakest bit of the article, which can cause a larger impact on the piece's rating than something further into the article. The later stuff is much more interesting though, and as I said I think that exchangers of secrets are an excellent fit for the universe.

Overall, interesting article with a slow start. Gave it a +1 after fixing the two leftover "SCP-XXXX"s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting concept, but this SCP is free to relocate itself at will, making it at least a "euclid" class object. It also presents a data-security risk. I feel like the Researcher here should be trying to negotiate with gold and gems instead of just freely offering up Foundation secrets.

item.title: Re: Well RIP
item.content:encoded:
Laneous,

Thank you. I do hope it stays up. It's nice to know that negative votes doesn't instantly mean it's getting taken down.

item.title: Re: Well RIP
item.content:encoded: Don't be so fatalistic, this only got posted today, and is at -2 with 13 votes. There's still a chance it'll stay up.

item.title: Well RIP
item.content:encoded: I guess this is getting taken down. I'm sorry most of yall didn't like it. Hopefully I do better next time!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just want to say that I find it funny that the SCP has zero ratings not because no one has read it, but because 6 people gave it a +1 and 6 people gave it a -1

item.title: Re: A review of SCP-4580
item.content:encoded:
Dr. P,

Thank you very much! I put a lot of work into this SCP, and I am happy to hear that you enjoy it!

item.title: A review of SCP-4580
item.content:encoded:
I like the way this is written. The clever usage (and proper vocabulary) of Norse Mythology has always been a favorite of mine. However, there were some phrases that were a bit unclear. During the segment between the doctor and the Ravens, a few sentences can be more properly ended by simply adding semicolons instead of commas. All in all, an interesting and great read.
Dr. P.
